1.1: The word of the LORD came to Jonah son of Amittai:
1.2: 'Get up and go to Nineveh, that great city, and proclaim against it, for their wickedness has come up before me.'
1.3: But Jonah got up to flee to Tarshish, away from the LORD's presence. He went down to Joppa and found a ship bound for Tarshish, paid the fare, and went aboard to sail with them to Tarshish—away from the LORD's presence.
1.4: But the LORD sent a powerful wind across the sea, and a violent storm arose so that the ship threatened to break apart.
1.5: The sailors were terrified, and each one called on his own god. They threw the cargo overboard to lighten the ship. But Jonah had gone down into the hold, where he lay fast asleep.
1.6: The captain went to him and said, 'How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will spare a thought for us and we won't die.'
1.7: Then the sailors said to each other, 'Let's cast lots to find out who is responsible for this disaster.' They cast lots, and the lot fell on Jonah.
1.8: They said to him, 'Tell us—who is responsible for bringing this disaster on us? What is your trade? Where are you from? What country and what people do you belong to?'
1.9: He answered, 'I am a Hebrew, and I worship the LORD, the God of heaven, who made both the sea and the dry land.'
1.10: This terrified them, and they said, 'What have you done?' For they knew he was fleeing from the LORD's presence, because he had told them.
1.11: They asked him, 'What must we do with you to make the sea calm down?' For the sea was growing rougher and rougher.
1.12: He told them, 'Pick me up and throw me into the sea, and it will calm down. I know it is my fault that this terrible storm has come on you.'
1.13: Instead, the men rowed hard trying to reach shore, but they could not, for the sea was surging against them.
1.14: Then they cried out to the LORD: 'Please, LORD, do not let us die for taking this man's life. Do not hold us guilty of innocent blood, for you, LORD, have done as you saw fit.'
1.15: Then they picked up Jonah and threw him into the sea, and the sea stopped raging.
1.16: The men were filled with deep reverence for the LORD, and they offered a sacrifice to him and made vows.
1.17: But the LORD appointed a great fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was inside the fish three days and three nights.

2.1: From inside the fish, Jonah prayed to the LORD his God.
2.2: He said: 'In my distress I called to the LORD, and he answered me. From the depths of Sheol I cried for help, and you heard my voice.'
2.3: 'You hurled me into the deep, into the very heart of the seas; the current surrounded me, and all your waves and breakers swept over me.'
2.4: 'I thought I had been banished from your presence; yet I will look again toward your holy temple.'
2.5: 'The water closed in over me to the point of death; the depths wrapped around me; seaweed was tangled about my head.'
2.6: 'I sank to the roots of the mountains; the earth with its bars shut me in forever. But you, LORD my God, brought my life up from the grave.'
2.7: 'As my life was ebbing away, I remembered the LORD, and my prayer reached you in your holy temple.'
2.8: 'Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the steadfast love that could have been theirs.'
2.9: 'But I will bring a sacrifice of thanksgiving to you; I will fulfill every vow I made. Salvation comes from the LORD.'
2.10: Then the LORD commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

3.1: The word of the LORD came to Jonah a second time:
3.2: 'Get up and go to Nineveh, that great city, and proclaim to it the message I give you.'
3.3: So Jonah went to Nineveh, just as the LORD had commanded. Now Nineveh was an enormously large city—it took three days to walk through.
3.4: On the first day, Jonah went into the city and proclaimed, 'Forty more days and Nineveh will be overthrown!'
3.5: The people of Nineveh believed God. They proclaimed a fast and put on sackcloth—from the greatest to the least.
3.6: When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robe, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat in the dust.
3.7: He then issued a proclamation throughout Nineveh: 'By order of the king and his officials: No person or animal, no herd or flock, is to eat or drink anything.'
3.8: 'Let people and animals be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let each person turn from their evil ways and from the violence they have committed.'
3.9: 'Who knows? God may yet relent and turn from his fierce anger, and we will not die.'
3.10: When God saw what they did—how they had turned from their evil ways—he relented and did not bring on them the disaster he had threatened.

4.1: But this greatly displeased Jonah, and he became angry.
4.2: He prayed to the LORD: 'LORD, isn't this exactly what I said before I left home? That is why I ran to Tarshish in the first place. I knew you were a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and overflowing with steadfast love—a God who relents from disaster.'
4.3: 'So now, LORD, please take my life—it would be better for me to die than to live.'
4.4: But the LORD replied, 'Is it right for you to be angry?'
4.5: Jonah left the city and sat down east of it. He built himself a shelter there and sat in its shade to see what would happen to the city.
4.6: Then the LORD God provided a leafy plant and made it grow up over Jonah to give shade for his head and to ease his discomfort. Jonah was overjoyed about the plant.
4.7: But at dawn the next day God sent a worm that chewed through the plant, and it withered.
4.8: When the sun rose, God sent a scorching east wind, and the blazing sun beat down on Jonah's head so that he grew faint. He wanted to die and said, 'It would be better for me to die than to live.'
4.9: But God said to Jonah, 'Is it right for you to be angry about the plant?' 'Yes,' he said, 'it is right—I am angry enough to die.'
4.10: But the LORD said, 'You are concerned about a plant that you did not tend or cultivate—it appeared overnight and died overnight.'
4.11: 'And should I not be concerned about Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 people who cannot tell right from wrong—and so many animals as well?'
